lines: In relation to the previous point, the required actions *Configure OTP* and *Update password* are moved in the order of required actions after *Verify Email*
required action. This is done automatically for new realm or during server update. If you prefer the old order, you can manually move required actions per your preference.
+Previous versions waited for one second for HTTP requests to finish during the shutdown of a node.

+The current version extends the timeout to ten seconds by default. In setups that use distributed caches, it now also waits for caches to rebalance before shutting down, preventing a possible data loss.

+When there are no ongoing HTTP requests, and caches are already rebalanced, the shutdown will finish immediately as before. With that, we expect most customers to not observe a change in the behavior of {project_name}.
+As before, for any rolling update or scaling up or down, we require to do this one node at a time for all clustered setups of {project_name}.
+Use the `shutdown-timeout` option to adjust the timeout to your environment. Restore the original behavior by setting it to `1s`.
=== Realm search now matches by display name
@@ -433,12 +433,13 @@ The server's readiness endpoint returns a "not ready" status, allowing the load
Existing TLS and HTTP keepalive connections are allowed to drain naturally.
The server continues to process existing requests.
-*Shutdown timeout*:: After the pre-shutdown delay, {project_name} stops accepting new requests and waits for in-flight HTTP requests to complete.
-If requests are still running after the timeout expires, the server shuts down regardless.
+*Shutdown timeout*:: After the pre-shutdown delay, {project_name} waits for in-flight HTTP requests to complete.
+Once the HTTP requests are complete, it uses the remaining time of the shutdown timeout to wait for caches to rebalance to avoid possible data loss.
+Once the timeout expires, the server shuts down regardless.
-By default, {project_name} is configured with a 1-second pre-shutdown delay and a 1-second shutdown timeout.
+By default, {project_name} is configured with a 1-second pre-shutdown delay and a 10-second shutdown timeout.
These defaults work well for most standard deployments where:
* The load balancer reconfigures quickly (within 1 second)
The shutdown period waiting for currently running HTTP requests to finish.
Both values accept duration formats: `1s` (seconds), `500ms` (milliseconds), `2m` (minutes), etc.

+ * Represents a condition that may block shutdown until it is resolved.
+ * Implementations define what "in progress" means (e.g. a cache rehash), and receive callbacks when the condition is
+ * resolved ({@link #complete()}) or when the shutdown timeout expires ({@link #onTimeout()}).
+     * @return {@code true} if the operation is still in progress and shutdown should wait.
+     * Invoked when the shutdown timeout expires while the condition is still {@linkplain #inProgress() in progress}.
+     * Invoked when the condition is no longer {@linkplain #inProgress() in progress} and shutdown can proceed,
+     * including when the condition was not in progress at the time of shutdown.

+ * Manages an ordered list of {@link ShutdownListener} instances that are notified sequentially when the server shuts
+ * Listeners are invoked in registration order. Each listener may block the calling thread to delay shutdown until its
+ * condition is met (e.g. waiting for a stable cache topology). All listeners share the same deadline, so the total
+ * shutdown time is bounded regardless of how many listeners are registered. It is recommended to respect it.
+ * If {@link #onShutdownStarted(Instant)} is called before {@link #onShutdown()} (e.g. from a shutdown delay event), the
+ * deadline is anchored to that earlier timestamp. Otherwise, it falls back to the current time when
+ * This class is thread-safe: listeners can be added or removed concurrently.
+    private static final Logger logger = Logger.getLogger(ShutdownManager.class);
+    private final List<ShutdownListener> listeners = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();
+     * Records the instant the shutdown sequence began (e.g. when the shutdown delay was initiated by Quarkus).
+     * @param shutdownStartTime The instant the shutdown was initiated.

+ * An Infinispan cache listener that delays server shutdown until the cache topology is stable (i.e. no rehash is in
+ * Use {@link #waitForStableTopology(Cache)} to register this listener on a cache. It returns a
+ * {@link WaitConditionShutdownListener} that should be added to the {@link ShutdownManager}.
+ * Limitation: when two or more Keycloak instances receive a shutdown signal simultaneously, each instance may
+ * observe a stable topology before any of them have actually left the cluster. In this scenario, all instances proceed
+ * with shutdown concurrently, increasing the risk of data loss.
+     * Registers a topology change listener on the given cache and returns a {@link WaitConditionShutdownListener} that
+     * blocks shutdown while the cache topology is unstable.
+     * @param cache         The cache to monitor for topology changes.
+     * @return A {@link WaitConditionShutdownListener} to be registered with the {@link ShutdownManager}.
+    private record TopologyShutdownCondition(String cacheName, DistributionManager dm) implements ShutdownCondition {

+            logger.warnf("Cache '%s': timed out waiting for stable topology during shutdown. Check for possible causes, or extend the shutdown timeout to allow for more time for the cache to rebalance.", cacheName);
+            logger.infof("Cache '%s': topology stable, proceeding with shutdown", cacheName);

+ * A {@link ShutdownListener} that blocks the shutdown thread until a {@link ShutdownCondition} is no longer
+ * {@linkplain ShutdownCondition#inProgress() in progress}, or until the provided deadline elapses.
+ * External events should invoke {@link #check()} whenever the condition may have changed (e.g. after a topology change
+ * event). If the condition has cleared, the waiting shutdown thread is unblocked and
+ * {@link ShutdownCondition#complete()} is called. If the timeout expires first, {@link ShutdownCondition#onTimeout()}
+    public void onShutdown(Instant shutdownTime, Date deadline) {
+     * Checks whether the {@link ShutdownCondition} is still in progress and, if not, signals the waiting shutdown
+     * This method should be called from external event handlers (e.g. topology change listeners) whenever the condition
+    private boolean awaitUntilStable(Date deadline) throws InterruptedException {
